data.title: Super Micro Computer FY Q2: Liquid Cooling And Blackwell Form A Killer Combination
data.description:
SMCI stock: previous thesis and FY Q2 recap

My last article on Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) was published about 1 month ago. To be more explicit, I published a Seeking Alpha article on Jan 23, 2024, under a title of “Super Micro Computer: Margin Expansion Potential Ahead”. In the article, I argued for a buy thesis for the stock due to my anticipation of margin expansion. Quote:

Since my last writing, the latest earnings projections on SMCI imply a margin expansion in the upcoming quarterly report. I do see various catalysts that could support such an expansion, with NVIDIA’s Blackwell chip availability as a leading one. There are still lingering concerns (such as delayed reporting). However, I see a favorable return/risk curve here due to the margin potential, low P/E ratio, and clarity provided by its internal auditing thus far.

A lot has happened to this battleground stock since that writing. First, the stock price went up by more than 80% since my last writing (see the next chart below), thus expanding its valuation drastically to a FWD P/E of 23x, compared to about 12x at the time of my last writing.

Second, the company has also released its quarter earnings report (ER) for FY Q2 on February 11, 2025. The results showed some unevenness with EPS missing consensus estimates but the topline exceeding consensus slightly. As one of the most hotly debated stock on the Seeking Alpha site, the ER was quickly reviewed by many SA authors. My overall view is that it helped to provide the much-needed clarity to address many of the ongoing market concerns, and it thus received overall bullish interpretations from many other SA authors.

Against this backdrop, the goal of this article is to examine an aspect that has received little discussion in the earnings reviews I’ve read thus far: the update the company provided on the development of its direct liquid cooling (DLC) system for Nvidia’s Blackwell AI chips. As detailed next, I think the DLC-Blackwell combination could drastically expand SMCI’s market share and thus reiterate my buy rating despite the substantial valuation expansion since my last writing.

SMCI stock: Liquid cooling ramps full production

SMCI has been working on liquid cooling technologies for years and has already reported encouraging progress before the FY Q2 report. As you can see from its investor’s presentation below, the company was already boasting a substantial rack production capacity in the end of 2024, manufacturing 5,000 racks per month, including over 2,000 liquid-cooled DLC racks. These racks are crucial for high-performance computing and can support high-power deployments ranging from 100kW, 150kW, and 200kW. During its Q2 2025 ER, CEO Charles Liang provided the following updates regarding its DLC capacity:

Liang announced that NVIDIA Blackwell products are shipping, including air-cooled and liquid-cooled GPU systems. Super Micro aims to expand its Datacenter Building Block Solutions to provide end-to-end data center services, reducing total cost of ownership for customers.

In its recent news release, SMCI also reported that it is ramping up full production of NVIDIA Blackwell Rack-Scale solution featuring liquid cooling. More details are quoted below.

SMCI news release (February 5, 2025): Supermicro Offers Next-Generation Air-Cooled and Liquid-Cooled Architecture for NVIDIA Blackwell Platform. SMCI is announcing full production availability of its end-to-end AI data center Building Block Solutions® accelerated by the NVIDIA Blackwell platform. The Supermicro Building Block portfolio provides the core infrastructure elements necessary to scale Blackwell solutions with exceptional time to deployment. The portfolio includes a broad range of air-cooled and liquid-cooled systems with multiple CPU options.

SMCI Stock: potential impact of LDC and Blackwell combination

My view is that the combination of liquid cooling and Blackwell chips has huge potential to change the fundamental cost structure of AI training and AI model development. To better illustrate my point, I gleaned the following numbers from a Forbes report to estimate the energy cost of AI development (the highlights were added by me):

  • The cost of the chips amounted to millions of dollars. According to a technical overview of OpenAI’s GPT-3 language model, each training run requires at least $5 million worth of GPUs.
  • These models require many, many training runs as they are developed and tuned, so the final cost is far in excess of this figure. When asked at an MIT event in July whether the cost of training foundation models was on the order of $50 million to $100 million, OpenAI’s cofounder Sam Altman answered that it was “more than that” and is getting more expensive.
  • The cost doesn’t end there. Running inference on the models, once trained, is also expensive. Estimates suggest that in January 2023, ChatGPT used nearly 30,000 GPUs to handle hundreds of millions of daily user requests. Sajjad Moazeni, a University of Washington assistant professor of electrical and computer engineering, says those queries may consume around 1 GWh each day.

Assuming an electricity price of ~$0.14 per kWh (which is about the mid-point of the national range of $0.12 to $0.17 in the U.S.), 1 GWh of electricity consumption per day translates into a cost of $140k. Multiplying this figure by 365 results in an annual cost of over $50 million – it is about 10x the AI chips upfront cost!

NVDA’s Blackwell AI chips can drastically lower the energy use (by as much as 25x) compared to its earlier H100 chips and/or many AI chips from other manufacturers while delivering about 30x higher throughput (see the next chart below). With the help from the liquid cooling racks from SMCI, these chips can operate at peak throughput for longer performance. With these improvements, the energy cost for training/deploying AI models would be reduced to a level that is far lower than the upfront cost of buying the hardware according to my foregoing analysis. I expect this to fundamentally change the cost structure, accelerate the expansion of AI applications, and also strengthen SMCI’s market share (well, Nvidia’s too at the same time).

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, as aforementioned, the stock’s valuation has expanded by about twofold since my last writing (from an FWD P/E ratio of 12x at the time of my last writing to the current 23x). For valued-oriented investors, a strong price rally means higher risks (even though momentum investor may see the opposite). SMCI’s current P/E ratios are certainly not cheap either by vertical or horizontal comparison. For instance, on a non-GAAP basis, SMCI's TTM P/E is 27.49, which is slightly higher than the sector median of 25.71 (by about 7%) but above its 5-year average of more than 42%. Its FWD P/E ratios are slightly lower than the sector median (by about 7 to 10%), but again above its 5-year average by about 33 to 45%.

Besides such valuation risks, SMCI is in a highly cyclical sector as IT expenditures responds sensitively to business cycles. Also, the sector is highly competitive too. Competition from other vendors (such as Dell and HP) could pressure SMCI’s margins and also its market share. Finally, SMCI’s reliance on Nvidia’s chip is a double-edged sword. My analysis above emphasized the positive side (which I think is the dominating force under current conditions). However, on the negative side, this reliance also makes SMCI vulnerable (e.g., to NVDA’s shifts of strategies and choice of suppliers).

All told, my verdict is that the upside potential far outweighs the downside risks under current conditions. I am particularly bullish about the progress the company reported about its liquid cooling racks in the earnings report. The combination of these racks with NVDA’s Blackwell chips is a catalyst that is underestimated (or even overlooked) by the market in my view. As such, I keep seeing a large skewness in its return/risk curve and maintain my buy rating.

data.title: Microsoft: Still Leading In Quantum Hardware
data.description:
This is my third article on Microsoft’s (NASDAQ:MSFT) plan to develop a quantum computer. In earlier articles, I suggested they were gaining a competitive advantage in the industry with their error mitigation qubit virtualization code and unique topological qubit research.

In this article, I suggest they are now pulling ahead of the competition in the second key area of scaling quantum technology in collaboration with Atom Computer and having shown the first processor based on its unique topological qubit, Microsoft has a clear path to a scalable error-free quantum machine.

The areas of error correction and scaling are the two technical hurdles that must be overcome before a quantum computer can compute problems beyond the ability of the classical computers we have today. Until those two problems are solved, the quantum hardware developers will not be able to generate significant sales, and the great promise of quantum computing will remain just a promise.

The general consensus, if disputed, is that scaled error-corrected quantum computers are still a decade or more away, and any investment in a company based on its quantum hardware must be considered extremely high risk.

Focusing on the hardware misses the industry's near-term revenue potential. It is not the hardware, it is the quantum ecosystem that could drive revenue in the short term.

[Please Note: This article is about Universal Gate Quantum Computing only, it does not cover the Quantum Annealing technology of D-Wave which is a completely separate technology on a different time line.]

The Timeline for Hardware

In a recent interview, Bill Gates said he regularly reviews the progress made by MSFT quantum computers and now believes they will possibly deliver a “quantum computer with enough true logical gates to solve some very tough problems in five years".

Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia (NVDA), said earlier this year, “If you said 15 years for very useful quantum computers, that would probably be on the early side,”

Mark Zuckerberg said in a recent interview with Joe Rogan that he believes quantum computers are more than a decade away.

The CEO of Rigetti (RGTI) said in a podcast interview on Yahoo! That the company is still in investment mode and that he does not expect material sales for five years.

The Alphabet (GOOG) CEO again stated that practical quantum computers are at least five to ten years away

So who is right? Is it 5 years or 15 years? Most likely, they are both right, but they are answers to different questions.

It will likely be 15 years before a large-scale error-corrected quantum computer is available to compute the difficult problems that the QC industry refers to. However, it might be only 5 years before quantum computers are providing data for classical AI that will lead to new scientific discoveries.

It could be an even shorter time frame for large revenue generation.

Quantum Ecosystems

Quantum computers do not and will not exist in isolation; they will be part of an ecosystem that includes High-Performance Computers (HPC) and artificial intelligence.

The original reason was to have the AI help people write the code needed for a quantum computer, and have the HPC manage the interaction with the quantum machine.

AI has seen an explosion in capabilities in the last 12 months. MSFT and others suggest that AI can generate meaningful results from the output of the current NISQ (Noisy Intermediate Scale Quantum) computers.

Current quantum machines are noisy; they make errors but they are quantum mechanical by nature and so can model quantum mechanical interactions. Despite knowing the data will have errors, Microsoft and others suggest that new advanced AI models could use the output from a quantum simulation as data. With enough data, the model could generate significant new scientific understanding.

There is no scientific evidence that this is true, indeed at a recent conference, the quantum scientist who coined the term NISQ John Preskill said

there is no proposed application of NISQ computing with commercial value……..Furthermore, currently there are no persuasive theoretical arguments indicating that commercially viable applications will be found

Quantum Today

It's not about the hardware and probably won't be for a decade or more, it's about the preparation. Billions of dollars will be spent preparing for our quantum future, and companies are scrambling to get a slice of the action.

The quantum ecosystem is the battleground, Microsoft is one major player but does not appear to be leading.

The sums of money involved are vast, in the short term all quantum companies can probably get a piece of the action. There will be over demand as almost every company, government and organization scrambles to get quantum ready and quantum safe and the short supply of quantum knowledge, skills and hardware will drive business for all quantum computing companies.

Quantum the industry imperative

Even if the promise of fault tolerant quantum computing is a decade or more away and the idea of using noisy quantum computers to generate data for AI turns out to be a dead end, there is a quantum imperative that means every company, organization and government must invest to ensure it is quantum safe and quantum ready.

The increased capability of a quantum computer is so great that it cannot be ignored, and companies that do not invest could be left so far behind that they cannot compete.

Quantum Safety

Today, most internet and wifi security is based on A-symmetric keys, a public key is freely distributed and a private key is kept separate. The private key is secured using mathematics that would take classical computers thousands of years to break. Shor’s algorithm, which runs on a quantum computer, will be able to work out the private key given the public key. Microsoft describes it as the trap door in modern internet security.

The imperative is clear: if you are not quantum-safe, your security will be cracked, if data is stolen now, even if encrypted, it will not be safe when quantum arrives.

Quantum FOMO

The Fear of Missing Out on Quantum is a tangible concern, no matter what the industry the arrival of a quantum computer could have profound effects. It would be capable of designing entirely new materials, ways of operating and efficiency savings on a scale we have not seen since the beginning of the industrial age.

Microsoft

The industry is pursuing multiple technologies for the qubits, the building blocks of quantum computing. One of these technologies will be the first to deliver a working machine; at that point, all the other technologies could be almost worthless. Still, the companies behind them may not be because of the value of their quantum eco systems. Of course, if a tech company, like Microsoft, is first to deliver a working quantum machine they would have an enormous advantage. Would they sell the machine to Google and IBM or keep it for their own cloud? It will be interesting to see, but it is an existential threat to every tech company.

Quantum Hardware

My first article on Microsoft covered the Majorana Zero Node particle research program and the mathematics behind it in detail. MSFT is attempting to develop a topological qubit using these elusive MZN particles. The project required new science and material discovery as the particles had never been proven to exist. They offer significant advantages over other approaches. Topological qubits are hardware error-protected and can be held close together allowing for scalability. This year's announcement of the first chip holding 8 topological qubits is groundbreaking for two reasons: firstly, they have proven the existence of a Majorana Zero Node particle, and secondly, have shown the ability to use and measure them as qubits. The project is still many years from completion and has already been running for 20 years but it remains the most elegant path to scalable, error-free quantum computing. Now that we know the qubit can be created and measured, this research avenue is the most promising, but it is far from being a slam-dunk.

In the linked topological qubit article, Microsoft reiterated the minimum for qubits

Whatever you’re doing in the quantum space needs to have a path to a million qubits. If it doesn’t, you’re going to hit a wall before you get to the scale at which you can solve the really important problems that motivate us

In my second article, I discussed Microsoft's quantum virtualization software that delivered the world's most accurate virtual/logical qubit to date. It was a ground breaking development and this year MSFT will use this technology and begin selling the world's first off-the-shelf quantum computer. This computer will be NISQ, prone to error and of insufficient scale to solve hard problems, but it is another step forward.

Last month, Microsoft and Atom Computing held a webinar to discuss the new computer, it covered a wide range of topics and explained the Microsoft NISQ roadmap in previously unseen detail. This section of the article relies heavily on the information released.

The Microsoft Quantum computer has been jointly developed with Atom, one of the world's leading neutral atom qubit companies. It will have 24 logical qubits using 100 of the available 1,000 neutral atom qubits in the Atom quantum chip lattice. Microsoft suggests the machine will scale as they improve the virtualization code and Atom guided to the next generation chip making 10,000 physical qubits available.

I wrote about neutral atom qubits in my article IONQ: Don’t buy the wrong qubit technology, I continue to believe that neutral atoms are superior to trapped Ion qubits and superconducting qubits. Neutral atoms have a huge benefit over other technologies, they are relatively easy to scale. Neutral atoms have no charge and can be held very close together, they can be packed into an array that should be scalable to millions of qubits without a significant change in the size of the machine. Other technologies require multiple quantum chips to be linked together and that is extraordinarily difficult to do, indeed it is a feat that has not yet been accomplished.

MSFT said they will need logical qubit fidelity of 99.999999999% to solve useful problems when they are currently only at 99.9%. They need to move from one error every thousand operations to one error every billion or quintillion.

Microsoft has proven that their logical neutral atom qubits have a higher fidelity than the physical qubits they are based on but it is still not good enough to perform the millions or billions of error free calculations. It might be the best but it is still nowhere near good enough.

In the webinar, Microsoft said they believe their machine will be able to provide data for their advanced AI models to reason with and mentioned the extraordinary speed at which AI technology is developing. The majority of the webinar involved discussing how the technology fits into the Quantum Azure ecosystem and how companies can use Azure to become quantum ready and quantum safe.

Microsoft Quantum Ecosystems

Quantum Azure is a full stack complete quantum computing package that will allow companies to learn how to develop quantum applications, what the potential is and prepare for the day they arrive. Within Azure is the discovery suite, this is the area for solving problems not doing quantum research. The Microsoft AI will help companies write quantum code and the HPC will run that code either in a simulated environment or on an actual quantum machine. It is an excellent learning tool and will be of immense value to companies beginning this journey.

The strategy seems clear and is to get companies and organizations tied into the Azure platform with the promise of quantum computing to follow. It is a good strategy but they are not the only ones following it. The advantage Microsoft does have is in the hardware. The topological qubit technology offers the greatest potential and the neutral atom qubits the best near term NISQ solution.

The Competition

Google

In December 2024 Google researchers announced a ground breaking finding, their new Willow chip had demonstrated “below threshold” error correction performance. The research paper published in Nature explained that the new Willow chip running a surface code to create logical qubits had demonstrated the possibility that as the number of qubits increased the error rate would be suppressed. At the press conference, the researcher Charina Chou raised the possibility that this improvement needed for full error correction could be reached within 10 years.

Google Ecosystem

Google is not yet offering a full-service quantum ecosystem, but it has a limited access offering.

Google's Quantum AI service is called Cirq, there is no public access and it is reserved to companies on an approved list. They have post-quantum cryptography research and help available for researchers but not end users it is focused on securing data on the google cloud.

The Google Cloud offers access to several different quantum machines, including IONQ and Riggetti, both companies are developing and deploying full stack ecosystems.

IBM

The new Heron chip has improved performance but represents a change in architecture, IBM will be looking to link multiple chips together rather than increase the number of qubits on each chip.

IBM continues to develop its surface code error correction and has reported progress with a tenfold improvement. It appears to be behind both Microsoft and Google with hardware, but there is a long way to go.

Ecosystem

IBM has a full stack quantum ecosystem called Qiskit. It is the most popular and best-performing offering, including HPC, AI and IBM's quantum computers.

IBM is heavily involved with using existing machines to generate data for AI, similar to Microsoft.

IBM quantum’s simulation of cosmological particles paper accepts that the machine is NISQ and so prone to error saying “ the lack of fault tolerance prevents the elusive dream of universal quantum computing,” The team developed a quantum system that could simulate the creation of particles and believe they have made interesting predictions even with the current level of noise and present a fairly substantial amount of data generated during the testing.

IBM may be in third place among the big three in hardware, but it appears to have the leading ecosystem likely to develop significant medium-term revenue. Indeed the IBM CEO guided to $1 billion of revenue already visible to the company from Quantum.

Amazon

Although not developing their own hardware they do have a full stack ecosystem called Amazon Braket that operates through AWS. The system links HPC, AI and a range of quantum computing hardware including three of the main qubit types. Superconducting qubits from Riggetti, trapped ion from IONQ and neutral atoms from QuEra. Amazon also has the Quantum Embark Program to help companies on AWS begin their quantum journey and become quantum safe.

Risks

The thesis of this article is that in the short term, companies will be spending heavily on quantum ecosystems as they prepare for the arrival of quantum computers. Microsoft, Google, Amazon and IBM have developed excellent offerings with IBM probably slightly ahead however it is likely that large organizations will stick with their current cloud provider until the status quo changes. If one of the tech companies develops a true fault tolerant computer then many customers could switch immediately to that provider. At the moment Microsoft appears to be ahead in this race but there are many years to go and it is too early to predict a winner or, in my mind, risk any money.

The thesis has one existential risk: a potential catastrophe for all of the companies mentioned is for someone else to develop a quantum computer before the expected time frame.

If it happened, it could signal a complete upheaval in the tech landscape, it is not as farfetched as one might think. There are two distinct possibilities: China and Psi.

PsiQuantum is a private somewhat secretive quantum computer hardware developer, and it is following a different path for the development of its qubits. Psi has big funding, having raised more than $1 billion at a valuation of $3 billion. This Palo Alto quantum computing company has the most aggressive timeline of all the companies. It has targeted the end of 2027 as the date it can deliver a large-scale error corrected quantum computer. That is two years away and perhaps 8 years ahead of the competition.

PsiQuantum claims it has achieved far more than released, which is a good thing as they have released very little. Psi uses light for its qubits, photons, to be more precise. Psi says it could launch a machine with one million qubits and full error correction in 2027. There is general skepticism in the scientific world. So far they have only demonstrated 2 physical qubits and the engineering challenge of dealing with these fragile probabilistic qubits is immense.

After DeepSeek, the advanced AI package that came out of nowhere we have to consider the possibility that Chinese companies or perhaps, Chinese state agencies are developing quantum Computers and recognize the possibility that they are ahead of the West.

I think both of these risks are very low probability.

Conclusion

Research to develop a reliable large scale quantum computer continues, the scientific literature is becoming flooded with new papers and research lines. The cold fact is that we have no evidence that any company is getting close to the goal, a universal gate computer that can solve problems beyond what current computers can solve.

Microsoft continues to develop its topological qubits which is the most ambitious of all attempts, it requires new materials and the ability to coax into life a particle that had never before been observed. This research continues a pace and is probably leading the race to the world's first actual quantum machine.

It is not certain that the technological problems will ever be overcome but the potential gains and threats of the technology are so great that billions of dollars is being spent preparing for its arrival. Quantum Computers can circumvent current cryptography systems and deliver improved versions of every material we currently use for manufacturing. No company or organization can ignore the possibilities or the threat.

Companies that can provide an ecosystem in which customers can learn the potential, mitigate the risks, and get themselves quantum-ready are beginning to generate substantial revenue. The quantum ecosystem will likely be a big part of future revenue for the major cloud providers and some smaller companies as they exploit the lack of quantum skills and capacity.

Disruption to the status quo will arrive with the first real quantum machine, at that point the technology industry is likely to go through a major upheaval and I will be monitoring this space closely for a suitable investment to make the most of this opportunity, I suspect I will have to wait a long time to make that investment.

For the time being I am rating Microsoft “industry perform” to do as well as its peers in this market, should the topological qubit take us out of the NISQ era they will warrant the strongest buy rating I have ever given but it is just too early to have any confidence in that outcome.

data.title: Gaia: Rebranding Success But Uncertain Road To Profitability
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After being rebranded from GAIAM Inc. to Gaia, Inc. (NASDAQ:GAIA) in 2016, it currently operates a streaming platform that caters to a niche interest group within the "conscious community". Such a rebranding exercise appears to have benefitted the company, as the company was reportedly one of the fastest-growing retailers in 2019.

Financially, while rebranding helped GAIA significantly diminish its loss in the bottom line, it has yet to achieve profitability. Its operating cash flow remains positive suggesting there are no immediate cash flow issues. GAIA maintains a manageable debt on its balance sheet. Although its accounts payable appears elevated, there are no reports of payment failures to its suppliers.

GAIA appears to demonstrate pricing power by increasing the price for all its offerings without materially losing any subscribers. However, such an increment was recent and unprecedented meaning investors should monitor whether the company's subscriber growth can be sustained in the long run.

The company is increasingly reliant on its latest, premium subscription tier of GAIA+ to generate subscription earnings. The earnings from GAIA+ are expected to eclipse its lower-tier subscriptions translating to higher Average Revenue Per User ('ARPU').

Despite its growth in certain performance indicators like subscriber growth and its ability to raise prices, its free cash flow remains consistently low and inconsistently leading to GAIA appearing highly overvalued using the discounted FCF model.

Investors should hold and observe whether the sustained growth in GAIA's subscriber counts can materially result in comparably sustainable growth in FCF.

Company Overview

GAIA provides digital spiritual and wellness-related content through a library of more than 10000 titles across multiple languages on its streaming platform. Customers access these contents through a subscription model.

The contents are organized primarily in 4 channels:

  • Yoga
  • Transformation
  • Alternative Healing
  • Seeking Truth

As opposed to dominant streaming platforms like Disney Plus and Netflix that seek to deliver entertainment services to their subscribers, GAIA targets a niche "conscious community".

Financials (Income Statement)

From the quarterly report:

Despite a visible increase in top-line revenue, the company still records a greater net loss due to an even greater increase in operating expenses.

The increase in expense is due to the absence of the "Employee Retention Tax Credit", a one-time government incentive to help businesses retain employees during the pandemic:

Financials (Balance Sheet)

GAIA's working capital is negative due to an outsized "Accounts payable" and "Deferred revenue":

Deferred revenue is due to the subscription-based model of GAIA's business, requiring subscribers to pay in advance before accessing the streaming service for the upcoming subscription period. Hence, in my opinion, it should not be a material concern.

A high account payable suggests GAIA's ability to negotiate longer payment terms from its suppliers. Since there were no reports of failed payments to its suppliers, it should not be a concern for now. Investors should keep a lookout for future potential failed payments from GAIA to its suppliers.

Debt accumulated is manageable at slightly more than 2x its operating cash flow:

Recurring net interest expense on its debt currently stands at less than 10% of its cash flow which I consider to be relatively low.

Financials (Cash Flow)

Despite its net loss, GAIA is still positive in operating cash flow after adjusting for non-cash expenses:

Despite the favourable operating cash flow reported in the latest 9-month period, the long-term trend of the company's FCF has been inconsistent:

Rebranding

The company was initially GAIAM Inc. when it rebranded to GAIA Inc. in 2016. This move to rebrand appears to benefit the company shortly after. In 2019, USA Today reported that GAIA is the "world’s 20 fastest growing retailers", with a sales growth rate reportedly higher than Amazon.

Looking at the visualization of GAIA's performance in operating income, the company used to be consistently loss-making before 2019. After 2019, although the company has yet to achieve consistent profits, its operating loss has significantly diminished.

While such a rebranding exercise successfully cut the loss of GAIA, it is unclear whether the company can improve its bottom line further to achieve consistent profits in the near future.

Despite the encouraging diminishing of net loss after the rebranding, from Seeking Alpha's visualization, GAIA's top-line revenue growth appears to have stagnated or decelerated in recent years, from 2021 to 2023. However, revenue improvement was achieved in the latest TTM raising the question of whether the latest improvement is sustainable.

Such an observation is in contrast to what the management claims during the earnings call that growth has been "sequential" for the last 5 years:

Even if there is growth as claimed by the management, the trend appears to be decelerating, except for the latest TTM period.

GAIA+ Premium Membership

The company's business seems to have grown from strength to strength after it was rebranded. Recently, it launched a more expensive subscription plan of GAIA+ offering subscribers more premium content than the basic plan:

Looking at the description of Gaia+, the extra services provided revolve around:

  • Workshops - hands-on interactive workshops hosted by subject-matter experts
  • GaiaSphere event - virtual events that are less hands-on than workshops but provide more insights into specific selected topics.
  • Gaia Marketplace - sells merchandise like books, apparel and health-related tools.

These offerings appear to be targeting existing customers who were already satisfied with the basic content and craved more personalized services from specific content providers in the GAIA platform.

As noted from the latest earnings call, the newer and more expensive GAIA+ subscription plan is expected to increasingly drive the company's overall membership growth in the future. At the current rate, the growth of GAIA+ subscriptions is likely to eclipse that of the lower-tier subscriptions:

In terms of overall membership growth, the management reported a high single-digit annual increase of 7% in the latest quarter:

Member count increments appear to be consistent with the previous 2 quarters' earnings calls which saw the membership count growing ~10%:

Q2 2024:

Q1 2024:

Pricing Power

GAIA seems to demonstrate some pricing power by increasing prices for its offerings, expecting such increment to translate to material growth in revenue:

Notably, while the immediate result of the price increment led to a short-term muted quarterly growth in membership growth, the management expects the growth to pick up in the next quarter:

Investors should monitor whether the company can achieve this target in the next earnings report.

Valuation

The following values taken from Seeking Alpha and other sources will be used in the intrinsic value ('IV') calculation using the discounted free cash flow model:

  • WACC as referenced from Guru Focus:

  • Starting FCF of $1.2M from the latest TTM period as calculated earlier.
  • GAIA's future is likely dependent on the continued growth of its subscriber base. The latest subscriber growth is observed to be close to 10%. Assuming the growth in subscribers translates to material growth in FCF at the same rate, I will model a scenario where FCF grows at 10% for the next 5 years.
  • Thereafter, the company sustains a terminal growth of 3%, which is within the "historical inflation rate (2-3%)"

With these assumptions, GAIA appears highly overvalued.

Conclusion

The company made remarkable strides in growth after its rebranding in 2016. However, it has yet to achieve profitability and its FCF remains low and historically inconsistent. Even with the optimistic assumption that its forward-looking FCF trend will remain positive and grow consistently, the company is still highly overvalued.

The key growth observations of rising subscriber count and assertion of pricing power have not materially proven to benefit its bottom line and FCF.

Investors who already have a position on GAIA at a price low enough to benefit from its recent appreciation should consider exiting.

Those who do not currently have a position should hold and wait for signs of sustained business growth that financially leads to material improvements in FCF.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: IPO Calendar Slows Down With Small Listings
data.description:
The calendar slowed down this past week, with only small listings in the US. One qualified for inclusion in our IPO stats.

Diagnostics company Anbio Biotechnology (NNNN) priced at the low end to raise $8 million at a $219 million market cap. Headquartered in Germany with international subsidiaries, Anbio develops in vitro diagnostics products for OTC, point-of-care settings, and laboratory applications. Its products are designed for an array of conditions. Revenue fell sharply in 2023 due to a decline in COVID-related products. Anbio finished up 15%.

While not included below, Wellgistics Health (WGRX) priced at the low end to raise $4 million at a $234 million market cap. Wellgistics finished down 15%.

1 IPO During the Week of February 17th, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Market Cap at IPO Price vs. Midpoint First Day Return Return at 02/21

Anbio (NNNN)

 $8M $219M -9% +15% +15%
Developer of in vitro diagnostics products.

There was one sizable filing this past week. Aerospace and defense company AIRO Group Holdings (AIRO) filed for a $100 million IPO.

7 Filings During the Week of February 17th, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Sector Lead Underwriter

AI Assets (AIAS)

 $9M Technology Univest Sec.
Provides an AI-enabled investment assistant app in Singapore.

AIRO (AIRO)

Provides aerospace, autonomy, and air mobility technologies.

Bgin Blockchain (BGIN)

Makes equipment for cryptocurrency mining with a focus on alternative currencies.

Visual content and effects producer in China.

Tong Ying (TYZ)

 $10M Industrials Joseph Stone
Hong Kong-based commodity trader focused on chemicals, metals, and agricultural products.

Blue Water Acq. III (BLUWU)

 $200M SPAC BTIG
Third blank check company led by the founder of Blue Water Venture Partners targeting healthcare.

CCIS Acquisition (CCACU)

 $60M SPAC Benjamin Securities
Blank check company targeting middle-market growth businesses.

Week Ahead

No IPOs are currently scheduled to list in the week ahead, although some small deals may list throughout it.

In the meantime, several sizable deals could launch in the coming week. Names on file eligible to launch include wearable cardiac device maker Kestra Medical Technologies (KMTS), eye disease biotech Aurion Biotech (AURN), industrial power system operator One Power (ONE), and autoimmune disease biotech Odyssey Therapeutics (ODTX).

Street research is expected for five companies in the week ahead, and three lock-up periods will be expiring.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 2/20/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 4.3% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 4.2%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Arm Holdings (ARM). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 9.0% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 7.0%. Renaissance Capital's International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Porsche.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Plummets To 15-Month Low Amid Tariff, Inflation Worries
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment plummeted to its lowest level since November 2023 this month, according to the final report for the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. The index dropped 6.4 points (-9.0%) to 64.7, marking the largest monthly decline since May and falling short of the 67.3 forecast. Sentiment is now down 15.9% from a year ago, the steepest annual decline since late 2022.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month; a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment extended its early month decline, sliding nearly 10% from January. The decrease was unanimous across groups by age, income, and wealth. All five index components deteriorated this month, led by a 19% plunge in buying conditions for durables, in large part due to fears that tariff-induced price increases are imminent. Expectations for personal finances and the short-run economic outlook both declined almost 10% in February, while the long-run economic outlook fell back about 6% to its lowest reading since November 2023. While sentiment fell for both Democrats and Independents, it was unchanged for Republicans, reflecting continued disagreements on the consequences of new economic policies.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 64.7, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today's report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 23.6% below its average reading of 84.6 (arithmetic mean) and 22.6% below its geometric mean of 83.5, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 8th percentile of the 566 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 3.3-point decrease from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here's a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I've included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In February, the CECI stood at 65.7, an 11.2% decline from the previous month and a 17.3% decline from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 68.7 and the largest monthly decline since May. Meanwhile, the CEI dropped to 64.0, reflecting a 7.6% decline from the prior month and a 14.9% drop from one year ago. This was also worse than the forecasted 67.3 and is the lowest level since November 2023.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations jumped up from 3.3% last month to 4.3% this month, the highest reading since November 2023 and marking two consecutive months of unusually large increases. The current reading is now well above the 2.3-3.0% range seen in the two years prior to the pandemic. Long-run inflation expectations rose over the course of the month and climbed from 3.2% in January to 3.5% in February. This is the largest month-over-month increase seen since May 2021. For both short- and long-run inflation expectations, this month's increases were widespread and seen across income and age groups. Inflation expectations rose this month for Independents and Democrats alike; they fell slightly for Republicans.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Smarter SaaS: Harnessing AI Agents For Sustainable Growth
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

Imagine a time when computers were solely used for basic calculations. Fast forward decades, and now they orchestrate symphonies of automation and intelligence across businesses. Today, AI agents represent the pinnacle of this evolution, autonomously handling tasks once thought impossible and redefining how software-as-a-service (SaaS) companies operate. In our view, SaaS represents the dominant business model for the various applications we think of as cloud computing companies.

Elon Musk has noted, "AI will be able to do everything." He envisions a future where AI's capabilities extend across domains, performing functions traditionally handled by knowledge workers and even reshaping the workforce entirely.1 Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella encapsulates this vision, stating, "AI agents will become the primary way we interact with computers in the future. They will be able to understand our needs and preferences, and proactively help us with tasks and decision-making."2 This highlights the transformative potential of AI agents in reshaping software interactions.

These AI agents are no longer confined to futuristic visions; they are actively driving efficiency and reshaping user experiences within SaaS platforms, becoming indispensable tools in modern business.

What Are AI Agents?

To understand AI agents, think about how they've quietly grown out of technologies we've come to rely on daily. Imagine scrolling through Netflix. The recommendations you see aren't random; they're the result of machine learning, analyzing what you've watched and guessing what you'll love next. Or consider the ads that pop up online - hyper-targeted to your recent searches and shopping habits. This evolution didn't happen overnight but has grown step by step, weaving intelligence into our digital lives.

Generative AI took this further. Tools like ChatGPT and DALL.E don't just analyze; they create. From drafting emails to designing prototypes, they've opened doors to creativity at scale. But even they are just stepping stones.

AI agents are the next leap. Unlike the single-purpose tools we're used to, these programs tackle interconnected tasks-managing customer service tickets, predicting sales trends or optimizing operations. They learn, adapt and make decisions independently, embodying a new level of software sophistication. They're not just tools; they're collaborators, reshaping what software can do for us.

How SaaS Companies Are Using AI Agents

In every corner of the SaaS world, AI agents are quietly revolutionizing the way companies operate-and the way we experience their products.

Take customer service. Not long ago, endless wait times defined support calls. Today, Zendesk's Answer Bot can handle routine inquiries instantly, 24/7.3 By the time a human agent joins, they're free to solve nuanced problems, creating better outcomes for customers and companies alike.

In sales and marketing, AI is the unseen strategist. HubSpot's (HUBS) tools analyze mountains of customer data, predicting who's ready to buy and crafting campaigns that speak directly to their needs. The results? More engagement, fewer wasted opportunities.

While those examples are certainly important, one of the biggest focal points over the past few years has been different AI solutions involving software coding. GitLab has recently enhanced its offering.

Imagine this: You're deep in the flow of coding. Ideas are spilling onto your keyboard faster than you can think, and you're building something new, something exciting. Then you hit a snag-a moment when a complex pattern or forgotten syntax halts your momentum. In that instant, GitLab's (GTLB) new AI solution can step in, like an experienced co-pilot who knows just when to take the controls.

GitLab's vision for AI in coding isn't just about convenience-it's about redefining how software is built. Its flagship solution, GitLab Duo Code Suggestions, does more than complete lines of code. It understands the rhythm of your work. With predictive code completion, it doesn't just offer snippets-it suggests whole functions, patterns and even the kind of nuanced structures that keep a coder in the zone. And it's not bound by one language; it speaks 14 fluently, from Python to Java, C++ to Ruby. Wherever you work-whether in GitLab Web IDE, VS Code or even NeoVIM-GitLab Duo fits in seamlessly.4

But there's more to the story. GitLab isn't walking this journey alone. It has partnered with Anthropic, a name synonymous with thoughtful AI. Anthropic's generative AI model, Claude, is woven into GitLab Duo's DNA, ensuring the code suggestions are not just smart but safe and ethical. This collaboration is about building trust-trust that the AI working alongside you has your back.5

Then there's GitLab's alliance with Amazon Q, which adds yet another layer of innovation. Imagine turning a vague feature idea into merge-ready code with just a few clicks. Picture AI-driven unit tests that not only save time but also elevate the reliability of your software. Or consider this: automated security reviews that catch vulnerabilities before they become problems. Amazon Q's integration is like giving your entire development workflow a turboboost.6

In the story of modern software development, GitLab is making a bold bet: that by weaving AI into the fabric of coding, we can build faster, safer and with more creativity than ever before. It's a story still being written, but one thing is clear: the future of coding isn't just human or machine-it's a partnership between the two.

Another example, recently highlighted within a featured discussion in Barron's, is Atlassian.7 Atlassian stands as a cornerstone in the world of team-collaboration software for developers, quietly weaving itself into the fabric of modern software development. Its reach is staggering: More than 15 million developers, representing more than 50% of all global developers, rely on its tools daily. More than 300,000 companies, from startups to industry titans, have integrated Atlassian into their workflows.

The numbers tell a compelling story. Over the past three years, Atlassian's (TEAM) revenue has grown at an annualized rate of 28%-a pace that few companies of its scale can match. The market now asks: Can this growth persist above 20% in a world where generative AI is poised to reshape the demand for software developers? While some see AI as a disruptor, threatening the need for human developers, the real story may be the opposite. AI, when combined with Atlassian's suite of tools, has the potential to supercharge productivity, solidify the company's position and extend the durability of its growth story.

A prime example of Atlassian's foresight is Rovo, an AI agent introduced in October 2024. Rovo doesn't just automate-it transforms. Tasks that previously consumed a day and a half each week are now completed in mere minutes, creating a ripple effect across software development teams. Beyond saving time, Rovo aligns with Atlassian's broader shift to cloud adoption, a move that not only deepens customer reliance but also unlocks long-term revenue growth. For a company already at the center of developer workflows through products like Jira and Confluence, Rovo represents an exponential leap forward.

Here's where the numbers get interesting. Without Rovo, Atlassian is expected to generate $8 to $9 in free cash flow per share by 2026. Add Rovo to the equation, and that figure could increase by $1 to $2 per share. At 35 times free cash flow-a reasonable valuation, given ServiceNow (NOW) trades at a premium-the stock could double in value. And that's with what could be considered conservative assumptions.

Atlassian's story isn't just about tools or technology. It's about leveraging the inevitable march of innovation to position itself as indispensable to the builders of tomorrow. If Atlassian can continue to marry foresight with execution, it won't just grow-it will thrive in ways that could redefine what's possible for the entire software ecosystem.

The Impact of AI on the Future of Work in SaaS

In the 1980s, an office was defined by the hum of typewriters, the shuffle of paper files and the steady rhythm of manual data processing. These tasks, monotonous yet essential, seemed irreplaceable. Fast forward to today, and those very jobs have been transformed-not eradicated-by the advent of computers and software. Instead of filing cabinets, we have cloud storage; instead of typewriters, digital platforms. Each wave of technological change didn't eliminate work; it shifted its nature, proving the Lump of Labor fallacy8-the belief that there is only a fixed amount of work to be done-wrong time and time again.

AI isn't here to replace us-it's here to augment us. The future of work in SaaS is not a zero-sum game; it's a dynamic partnership. Humans will be freed from mundane tasks to engage in roles that require empathy, creativity and strategic thinking. This shift is already fostering new careers in AI development, data science and experience design. The workforce isn't shrinking; it's evolving-proving once again that technology doesn't reduce our purpose but refines it for the better.

Conclusion: AI Agents as Growth Catalysts for SaaS

The journey of AI agents in SaaS is more than just a story of automation; it's a narrative of unlocking untapped potential. For SaaS companies grappling with the challenge of balancing growth and profitability, AI agents provide a bridge. Imagine a company struggling to scale-AI doesn't just streamline operations; it identifies new markets, crafts personalized customer experiences and amplifies the effectiveness of every sales interaction. This isn't hypothetical; it's the new reality for forward-thinking SaaS leaders.

Recent findings by McKinsey highlight a critical lesson: SaaS companies that overcorrect toward profitability risk leaving substantial value on the table.9 AI agents could represent an antidote to this trend. By automating repetitive tasks and augmenting human decision-making, AI not only reduces operational costs but also fuels innovation and growth. From smarter marketing campaigns to seamless product development cycles, the possibilities are vast.

Looking ahead, AI agents could be the key to unlocking better returns in software stocks. As these tools continue to evolve, they are set to redefine not just the SaaS industry but the expectations of software's role in driving business value. The companies that embrace this technology strategically, balancing efficiency with investment in growth, will lead the next wave of SaaS transformation-creating enduring value for their shareholders and customers alike.

Investing in the AI-Powered Future of SaaS

As AI agents continue to redefine software development, productivity and efficiency, the companies leading this transformation stand to benefit the most. For investors looking to capture the potential of AI-driven SaaS growth, the WisdomTree Artificial Intelligence Fund (WTAI) provides targeted exposure to innovative businesses at the forefront of this revolution.

Footnotes

1 Schappert, Stefanie. "Musk Predicts 'More Robots than People' by 2040 in Latest Interview. Here's What Else He Said from AI to Zed." CyberNews, 29 Oct. 2024.

2 "AI Agents Will Revolutionise SaaS and Productivity: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella." Economic Times, 7 Jan. 2025.

3 Customer Service Answer Bot. Zendesk, Chatbot for customer service | Zendesk

4 GitLab Duo Documentation. GitLab, GitLab Duo

5 Chu, Kevin. "GitLab Uses Anthropic for Smart, Safe AI-Assisted Code Generation." GitLab Blog, 16 Jan. 2024.

6 Guimaraes, Matheus. "Introducing GitLab Duo with Amazon Q." AWS News Blog, 3 Dec. 2024.

7 Rublin, Lauren R. "Nvidia, Smucker, Muni Bonds, and 31 Other Investment Ideas From Our Roundtable Pros." Barron's, 17 Jan. 2025. Comments on Atlassian from Henry Ellenbogen's portion of the Barron's roundtable discussion.

8 Nowak, et al. The Morgan Stanley AI Guidebook: Third Edition. Morgan Stanley Research, 18 Oct. 2023.

9 How Efficient Growth Can Fuel Enduring Value Creation in Software. McKinsey & Company

Important Risks Related to this Article

Some or all of the companies mentioned may be held in the WTAI. For a list of the top 10 holdings and a complete list of holdings, please click here.

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. The Fund invests in companies primarily involved in the investment theme of Artificial Intelligence and Innovation. Companies engaged in AI typically face intense competition and potentially rapid product obsolescence. These companies are also heavily dependent on intellectual property rights and may be adversely affected by loss or impairment of those rights. Additionally, AI companies typically invest significant amounts of spending on research and development, and there is no guarantee that the products or services produced by these companies will be successful. Companies that are capitalizing on Innovation and developing technologies to displace older technologies or create new markets may not be successful. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund's prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund's risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree's London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU's Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor's degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

data.title: HSBC Holdings Continues To Reward Shareholders
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The largest position in our portfolio is HSBC Holdings plc (NYSE:HSBC), due to its share price going up by 39% over the last 12 months.

In August last year, we shared our view on their FH 2024 financial results. We continued a string of Buy stances. One would have to return to 2019 to find our neutral stance with a hold stance.

Towards the end of the pandemic, we shared with the SA community that it was time to buy, as we stated that HSBC was "extremely cheap". The price was $21.39 per ADR and has since returned 240%. It even surpassed the great-performing S&P 500 which delivered 82.6%.

But we cannot rest on our laurels. The past is the past. We are investing for the future.

With the ADR now standing at $56.88 is it time to sell, hold, or even buy at this level?

Let us look under the hood.

HSBC FY 2024 Financial Results

Net profit for FY 2024 was $25 billion, which was an improvement of $440 million, or 1.8%. This works out to an EPS of $1.24 per share. The ADR consists of 5 ordinary shares. As such, the earnings per ADR was $6.2. With a share price of $56.96, we get a P/E of 9.2

In the context of the S&P 500 P/E being 27.55, HSBC is still attractively priced. A more relevant comparison would be with JPMorgan Chase (JPM) which has a P/E of 15.3

Another way to look at valuation is the price to Tangible Net Book Value. Here, we will see that HSBC has been able to increase the NTBV over the last couple of years. As shareholders, we like that too.

JPM’s P/B is as high as 2.41 on a TTM basis.

As we usually remind our readers, the two most important sources of income for banks are net interest income and fees.

HSBC’s NII decreased by as much as $3.1 billion to $43.8 billion last year. That is a drop of 6.6%

To mitigate reductions in NII, HSBC has hedged large parts of the sensitivity towards the NII. As of the end of 2024, this hedge had a balance of $529 billion.

We have to assume that the net interest margin peaked in 2023.

The Return on Tangible Equity, or ROTE, remains good at 14.6%. It was the same as in 2023.

After the GFC of 2008, all banks are holding more equity. HSBC’s Common Equity Tier-1 ratio is stable at 14.9%

We always like to monitor their Estimated Credit Losses. In this latest FY, the ECL came in at $3.4 billion.

$1.8 billion came from their Commercial Banking division and $0.2 billion came from Global Banking and Market. This included $0.4 billion as stage 3 charges relating to the commercial real estate sector in mainland China and $0.1 billion from the Hong Kong real estate sector.

A charge of $ 0.1 billion came from one single CMB customer in the UK. The ECL from the division of Wealth and Private Banking was quite large at $1.3 billion.

It is important to look at ECL in conjunction with the size of their total loan book. The ECLs are only 36 bps of the average gross loans. That was an increase of 4 bps from FY 2023.

Returning capital to shareholders

This is important to investors. Not just for us, but also for one of their largest shareholders, Ping An Insurance of P.R.C. In 2022 they became very vocal about HSBC's low payout ratio. They also wanted the bank to separate the Asian business from the rest in two separate companies. That they did not succeed with.

But, a more shareholder-friendly return of capital to shareholders did commence.

That is also one of the reasons we have seen a revival in the share price over the last couple of years.

The dividend for FY 2024 is $0.87 per share, or $4.35 per ADR.

On a TTM basis, we get a dividend yield of 7.6%. That is more than 4 x JPM’s 1.7% dividend yield. For foreign investors, like ourselves, we do not pay tax on dividends, and since it is the same in Hong Kong, where we own the HSBC shares, we get dividends without any withholding tax. If we were to own JPM in the U.S., we would have to pay a 30% withholding tax before receiving dividends in our bank account. The difference is huge.

On top of the dividend, HSBC bought back 9% of their shares in 2024 and commenced another buyback program to buy back up to $2 billion of their shares within the first quarter of this year.

$47.7 billion is a large amount of money. If we calculate what that means on an average yearly basis we “received” $1.33 per share.

That is in effect a yield of 12% per year.

Risk to Thesis

One risk is an increase in ECLs. This could come with an increase in property mortgages ending up in their Stage 3 bucket. However, we do not think that this is very likely.

For mortgages in their ring-fenced UK market, the average LTV was 53%, and for Hong Kong, it was 63% at the end of 2024. This is somewhat higher than the 50% and 57% LTV recorded in their 2022 annual report. It is still giving the bank good buffers for further drops in real estate prices.

Other risks, which all banks have to deal with are from their trading activities.

We as investors face the risk of a lower share price for HSBC and a reduction in their dividends.

Conclusion

After having gone through most of their 460-page annual report, based on our limited understanding and our belief in good faith, we still think that HSBC is good value at the present share price.

We do not want to give a price target, but it would still give a yield of 5.6% and have a P/E of a reasonable 12.3 if the share price for the ordinary shares trading in Hong Kong was HKD 120. That is 50% higher than what it is now.

Therefore, we believe that there is still upside potential from here on.

Our buy stance remains.

data.title: Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Hawaiian Electric Industries, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: PVAL: A Low Valuation Fund Outperforming Peers
data.description:
The Putnam Focused Large Cap Value ETF (NYSEARCA:PVAL) is an actively managed fund with a strong value profile and a selective approach to building a portfolio that has been delivering solid performance compared to other value funds.

While the current high valuations seen in the overall market strengthen the case for value-oriented investment strategies, it is interesting to watch whether PVAL can continue to outperform, despite its low exposure to technology, a sector that has led the bull market in recent years.

ETF Description & Highlights

PVAL is an ETF that provides exposure primarily to U.S. large-capitalization companies with value characteristics. It is an actively managed fund that selects companies with market capitalization similar to the Russell 1000 Value index, which is a subset of the parent Russell 1000 index, a typical benchmark for large- and mid-cap stocks.

PVAL’s portfolio consists of companies that the fund’s manager believes are undervalued in the market, taking into consideration aspects such as valuation metrics, financial strength, growth prospects, competitive position in the respective industry, future earnings forecast, cash flows, and dividends.

As of January 31, 2025, PVAL had $2.1 billion in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 44 companies, with an average market cap of $115.1 billion. The allocation is skewed toward large caps, as expected, with 41.6% of total assets, while the allocation to mid caps is also representative, accounting for 34.4% of the fund, followed by mega caps at 20.0% and a marginal exposure to small caps, which make up 1.3%. In contrast, the Russell 1000 index, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), has a significantly higher allocation to mega and large caps, with 42.9% and 32.8%, respectively, reflecting a market cap-weighted approach that emphasizes larger companies.

PVAL's top ten holdings are Citigroup (4.51%), Walmart (4.03%), Bank of America (3.92%), Exxon Mobil (3.47%), Capital One (3.45%), Thermo Fisher Scientific (3.36%), Oracle (3.25%), UnitedHealth Group (2.97%), Hilton Worldwide (2.89%), and AIG (2.74%), which together represent 35% of total assets. The fund’s top holdings are weighted toward value-driven sectors like financial services (Citigroup, Bank of America, Capital One, and AIG) and healthcare (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UnitedHealth Group), which alongside holdings in consumer staples (Walmart) and energy (Exxon Mobil), reinforce the fund’s core investment strategy.

The following table includes a list of large-cap funds focused on the value-oriented strategies that will serve as the benchmark for PVAL in this analysis. The first one is the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV), the largest fund in the group, managing $135 billion and employing a multi-factor approach. Next is the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), derived from the Russell 1000 index, which is also a large fund with $64 billion in assets under management and a significant allocation to mid-cap stocks. The iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) utilizes three fundamental factors to select value equities from the S&P 500 index. Another key player, the iShares Core S&P US Value ETF (IUSV), is a subset of the S&P 900 index, while the Schwab US Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV) follows a market cap-weighted approach with a strong bias toward mid caps. Lastly, the Vanguard Mega Cap Value ETF (MGV) specifically targets mega-cap stocks, with a relatively concentrated portfolio of 137 holdings.

From a sector allocation perspective, PVAL's largest allocation is to the financial services sector, with 22.1% of total equities, followed by healthcare at 15.4%, consumer staples at 11.7%, industrials at 11.5%, technology at 10.0%, consumer discretionary at 8.2%, energy at 7.1%, utilities at 5.2%, basic materials at 4.5%, communication services at 2.7%, and real estate at 1.7%. Relative to the Russell 1000 index, PVAL is overweight in financial services (+8.2%), consumer staples (+6.3%), and healthcare (+5.3%), but underweight in technology (-21.7%), communication services (-7.0%), and consumer discretionary (-3.1%).

The fund has a selective allocation to financial services, its largest sector, but with holdings across different industries, such as global banks Citigroup and Bank of America, investment banking Goldman Sachs, asset manager Apollo Global, credit provider Capital One Financial, and insurer AIG.

Meanwhile, PVAL’s holdings in consumer staples, PVAL’s second-largest sector, focus on three core industries: hypermarkets (Walmart and BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings), soft drinks (Coca-Cola), and tobacco (Philip Morris International), while the fund’s allocation to industrials, another core sector for PVAL, includes companies from different segments, such as defense and aerospace (Northrop Grumman), logistic (FedEx), low-cost airline ( Southwest), and equipment manufacturer (Ingersoll Rand).

When compared to the peer group, PVAL is slightly overweight in consumer staples (+2.8%), basic materials (+1.7%), and financial services (+1;0%), as it has the largest exposure to consumer staples and basic materials among all peers. On the other hand, PVAL is underweight in technology (-6.0%), real estate (-1.6%), and communication services (-1.1%), as funds IVE and IUSV maintain a somewhat aggressive allocation to technology for a value fund at the 24% level.

Low Valuations

PVAL's average price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) of 15.6x represents a roughly 30% discount to the Russell 1000 index, with about half of this valuation gap due to PVAL’s underweight exposure to high-valuation sectors like technology and consumer discretionary, while PVAL’s stock selection also plays an important role here. This is evidenced by the low valuation of some of the fund’s core holdings in sectors such as consumer discretionary (PulteGroup: P/E 8.3x, General Motors: P/E 5.3x), technology (Oracle: P/E 27.4x, Qualcomm: P/E 14.1x), and healthcare (UnitedHealth: P/E 18.5x, McKesson: P/E 17.7x).

The fund’s earnings growth profile is lower than the Russell 1000 index, driven by its low exposure to high-growth sectors such as technology, alongside modest growth in some of the fund’s top holdings, such as Citigroup (EPS Fwd: 1.3%), Capital One (EPS Fwd: -4.1%), and Thermo Fisher Scientific (EPS Fwd: 0.3%),

On the profitability side, PVAL’s average EBITDA margin of 26.5% is nearly six percentage points lower than the Russell 1000 index, reflecting its low exposure to big tech names, while core holdings such as Walmart and McKesson are typically low-margin businesses with EBITDA margins below 10%.

PVAL’s P/E ratio is the lowest among peers, with an average 10% discount to the peer group. This valuation gap is largely driven by PVAL’s low exposure to technology, especially relative to funds IVE and ISUV, as their nearly 24% allocation to this sector is reflected in their P/E ratios in the range of 18%.

Outperforming The Peer Group

PVAL total returns over the past three years have been slightly higher than the Russell 1000 index, as the strong outperformance in the bearish year of 2022 more than offset weaker performance in the following two years, as broader equity indexes delivered solid gains on the heels of a strong bull market. This pattern is largely expected, as value-oriented funds like PVAL tend to outperform during negative years but have struggled to keep pace with the overall market in a more bullish environment, given the recurrent outperformance of growth stocks over the past years. Compared to the peer group, PVAL has outpaced all funds in this peer group year after year since its inception in May 2021, with the exception of 2022, when it ranked third, as MGV and VTV were more resilient that year, showing smaller losses during that period.

PVAL’s volatility has been lower than the Russell 1000 index, as expected for a value fund, while its beta of 0.91 was in line with most peers, just above MGV, VTV, and SCHV.

PVAL’s average spread of 0.04% is arguably low, driven by a reasonable average trading volume of $10 million, while other value funds have shown spreads as low as 0.01% and 0.02%, thanks to their heavy volume in general. On the other hand, its turnover of 54% is the highest in the peer group, leading to an expense ratio of 0.56%, which is much higher than peers, likely due to the fact that it is an actively managed fund. PVAL’s dividend yield of 1.28% is the lowest in the peer group, reflecting its low exposure to real estate and its stock picking in healthcare and consumer staples. On the other hand, PVAL’s tracking error of around 7% is in line with peers, while its adjusted returns, as measured by its Sharpe ratio of 0.61 for the three-year period is the highest in the group, driven by strong returns and volatility in line with peers.

Final Thoughts

PVAL’s investment approach favoring undervalued companies is reflected in a low price-to-earnings ratio of 15.6x, which is a positive for the fund, given the currently stretched valuations in the overall market, as it is likely to limit downside risk in the event of a stock market correction.

In addition, with a concentrated portfolio of 44 holdings compared to peers, the fund’s active management philosophy is expected to have played a central role in its stock selection, as evidenced by a high turnover of 54% for a value fund. Nevertheless, it is fair to say that its investment style appears to have added value to the fund over this relatively short time since its inception in May 2021, given its outperformance compared to other value funds over this period. This happened despite PVAL’s underweight allocation to the technology sector, which has been one of the biggest winners over the past few years.

In summary, while investors should bear in mind that relatively concentrated portfolios like PVAL may be subject to periods of underperformance, due to potential lower correlation to broader equity indexes, my take on PVAL is overall positive, and I view it as one of the good funds to hold for the value-oriented portion of an investor’s portfolio.
